steps.text: Train each employee involved in operating a process in an overview of the process and in the operating procedures.
Provide refresher training at least every three years or more often if necessary to each employee involved in operating a process.
Prepare a record containing the identity of the employee, the date of training, and the means used to verify understanding of the training.
Assure that each contract employee is trained in the work practices necessary to perform his/her job.
What are the program’s main strengths?
What are the program’s main weaknesses?
What is recommended to improve the program?
Are instructors instructing according to their training outlines?
Is the evaluation tool current and appropriate for the program content?
Is the course material current and relevant to the target group?
Off-site training. Training course content for hazardous waste operations should include the following topics or procedures:
Regulatory knowledge.
A review of 29 CFR 1910.120 and the core elements of an occupational safety and health program.
The content of a medical surveillance program as outlined in 29 CFR 1910.120(f).
The content of an effective site safety and health plan consistent with the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.120(b)(4)(ii).
Emergency response plan and procedures as outlined in 29 CFR 1910.38 and 29 CFR 1910.120(l).
Adequate illumination.
Sanitation recommendation and equipment.
Review and explanation of OSHA’s Hazard Communication standard and Lockout/Tagout standard.
Review of other applicable standards including but not limited to those in the Construction standards.
Ensure employees have received proper training in the various precautions and safe practices required by this section.
Provide training consisting of on-the-job training or classroom-type training or a combination of both where required.

Certify that employees have been trained by preparing a certification record which includes the identity of the person trained, the signature of the employer or the person who conducted the training, and the date the training was completed.
Maintain the certification record on file for the duration of the employee’s employment.
Make the certification record available upon request to the Assistant Secretary for Occupational Safety and Health.
Include training topics such as recognition and avoidance of dangers relating to encounters with harmful substances and animal, insect, or plant life.
Include procedures to be followed in emergency situations in training.
Include first-aid training, including instruction in artificial respiration.
Ensure only persons trained in the operation of the derrick shall be permitted to operate the derrick.
Inform employees of the content of Appendices A and B of this regulation.
Train each employee who is subject to exposure to lead at or above the action level.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.
Provide initial training by 180 days from the effective date for those employees covered by paragraph (l)(1)(ii).
Repeat the training program at least annually for each employee.
Inform each employee of the content of this standard and its appendices.
Inform each employee of the specific nature of the operations which could result in exposure to lead above the action level.
Inform each employee about the purpose, proper selection, fitting, use, and limitations of respirators.
Inform each employee of the purpose and a description of the medical.
Provide employees with information and training at the time of their initial assignment to a work area where benzene is present.
Provide information and training at least annually thereafter if exposures are above the action level.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program for employees in a regulated area.
Provide the training program at least annually.
Make employees familiar with the actual sound of the immediate evacuation warning signal by demonstration.
Inform all individuals working in radiation areas about the existence of radioactive materials and the associated safety problems.
Instruct employees on precautions or devices to minimize exposure to radiation.
Advise employees to request reports of radiation exposure as per regulations.
Provide effective information and training on hazardous chemicals at the time of initial assignment.
Inform employees about any operations in their work area where hazardous chemicals are present.
Ensure each employee involved in operating a process receives refresher training at least every three years.
Prepare a record containing the identity of the employee, the date of training, and the means used to verify that the employee understood the training.
Train each contract employee in the work practices necessary to safely perform his/her job.
Instruct each contract employee in the known potential fire, explosion, or toxic release hazards related to his/her job and the process.
Document that each contract employee has received and understood the training required.
Prepare a record containing the identity of the contract employee, the date of training, and the means used to verify that the employee understood the training.
Confirm that training of each employee involved in operating a process has been completed prior to the introduction of highly hazardous chemicals.
Train each employee involved in maintaining the ongoing integrity of process equipment in an overview of the process and its hazards.
Review and explanation of OSHA’s hazard-communication standard (29 CFR 1910.1200) and lock-out-tag-out standard (29 CFR 1910.147).
Review and knowledge of confined space entry procedures in 29 CFR 1910.146.
Work practices to minimize employee risk from site hazards.
Safe use of engineering controls, equipment, and any new relevant safety technology or safety procedures.
Review and demonstration of competency with air sampling and monitoring equipment that may be used in a site monitoring program.

Container sampling procedures and safeguarding; general drum and container handling procedures including special requirement for laboratory waste packs, shock-sensitive wastes, and radioactive wastes.
The elements of a spill control program.
Proper use and limitations of material handling equipment.
Review of the principles and practices for analyzing an incident
Review of procedures for implementing continuing response actions consistent with the local emergency response plan
Review of the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment
Review of the principles and practices of establishing exposure zones, proper decontamination and medical surveillance stations and procedures
Review of the expected hazards including fire and explosions hazards, confined space hazards, electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards

Awareness and knowledge of the competencies for the Hazardous Materials Technician covered in the National Fire Protection Association’s Standard No. 472, Professional Competence of Responders to Hazardous Materials Incidents
Review of and demonstration of competency in performing the applicable skills of 29 CFR 1926.65(q)
Hands-on experience with retrieval and use of written and electronic documents
Erect, move, dismantle, or alter scaffolds under the supervision of a competent person.
Ensure activities are performed by experienced and trained employees selected by the competent person.
Train each employee who performs work on a scaffold.
Train employees to recognize the hazards associated with the type of scaffold being used.
Train employees to understand the procedures to control or minimize those hazards.
Include training on electrical hazards, fall hazards, and falling object hazards.
Include training on correct procedures for dealing with electrical hazards and for maintaining fall protection systems.
Include training on the proper use of the scaffold and materials handling.
Include training on the maximum intended load and load-carrying capacities of scaffolds.
Include any other pertinent requirements of the subpart.
Train each employee who is potentially exposed to cadmium in accordance with the requirements of this section.
Institute a training program.
Ensure employee participation in the program.
Maintain a record of the contents of the training program.
Provide training prior to or at the time of initial assignment to a job involving potential exposure to cadmium and at least annually thereafter.
Make the training program understandable to the employee.
Inform each employee of the health hazards associated with cadmium exposure.
Inform each employee of the quantity, location, manner of use, release, and storage of cadmium in the workplace.
Inform each employee of the engineering controls and work practices associated with the employee's job assignment.
Inform each employee of the measures they can take to protect themselves from exposure to cadmium.
Inform each employee of the purpose, proper selection, fitting, proper use, and limitations of respirators and protective clothing.
Inform each employee of the purpose and a description of the medical surveillance program.
Inform each employee of the contents of this section and its appendices.
Inform each employee of their rights of access to records under 1926.33(g)(1) and (2).
Provide additional access to information and training program materials.
Know and understand the type of signals used.
Be competent in the application of the type of signals used.
Have a basic understanding of equipment operation and limitations.
Know and understand the relevant requirements of 1926.1419 through 1926.1422 and 1926.1428.
Demonstrate that he/she meets the requirements in paragraphs (c)(1) through (4) through an oral or written test, and through a practical test.
Train each employee specified in 1926.1408(g) and 1926.1410(m) in the topics listed in 1926.1408(g).
Train each employee who will be assigned to work as a signal person who does not meet the requirements of 1926.1428(c) in the areas addressed in that paragraph.
Train each operator-in-training in the areas addressed in 1926.1427(j).
Provide re-training if the operator-in-training does not pass a qualification or certification test.
Train each operator who has not yet been certified or qualified in the areas addressed in 1926.1427(j).
Train each operator excepted under 1926.1427(a) on the safe operation of the equipment the operator will be using.
Train each operator of the equipment covered.

preconditions.text: Before training employees in a newly assigned process, ensure they are trained in an overview of the process and in the operating procedures.
